Journal: Journal of Geochemical Exploration

DOI: 10.1016/j.gexplo.2026.108123

Relevance: 0.570 (consider)

Porphyry molybdenum (Mo) deposits are the world's primary source of Mo and form through multi-stage processes involving source melting, magmatic differentiation, and fluid exsolution that drives hydrothermal Mo mineralization. The transition from magmatic to hydrothermal conditions is a critical phase in these magmatic-hydrothermal systems, yet its primary features are often obscured by subsequent overprinting, hindering direct observation. Biotite preserves a continuous record from magmatic crystallization to hydrothermal alteration, providing critical insights into this evolution. To quantitatively reconstruct the magmatic-hydrothermal transition and elucidate Mo enrichment mechanisms, this study utilizes in-situ geochemical analyses of various biotite generations from the Chaijiagou porphyry deposit, northern North China Block (NCB). Based on quantitative geochemical criteria, three biotite types were identified: early-stage magmatic biotite (Bt1), late-stage magmatic biotite (Bt2), and hydrothermal biotite (Bt3). Magmatic biotite shows a pseudo-A-type geochemical signature, indicating an extensional geodynamic setting related to lithospheric thinning of the NCB. Magmatic biotite crystallized at an average depth of 4.7 ± 0.7 km and temperature of ~705 ± 5 °C, under relatively oxidized conditions (ΔFMQ = +0.13 to +0.31, average 0.25 ± 0.03), which limited early sulfide saturation and promoted initial Mo enrichment. Progressive melt evolution through fractional crystallization is evidenced by systematic decreases in K/Rb ratios from Bt1 (76.8–281.1) to Bt2 (63.8–116.9), accompanied by enrichment in incompatible elements. Low halogen intercept values in magmatic biotite indicate an initially F-rich magmatic system that enhanced melt fractionation and metal fertility. During the magmatic-hydrothermal evolution, hydrothermal biotite (Bt3) records a shift toward high F fugacity, with IV(F) increasing to 1.66–1.74, IV(F/Cl) rising to 6.24–6.44, and IV(Cl) decreasing to −4.71 to −4.56. Meanwhile, log(f H2O /f HF ) ratios dropped from 2.02 to 2.04 in the magmatic stage to approximately 1.47 ± 0.03 in the hydrothermal stage. This elevated F activity significantly enhanced the solubility and transport capacity of Mo complexes. Large-scale molybdenite precipitation was triggered by cooling to ~400 ± 3 °C, coupled with previously documented fluid boiling and meteoric water influx, which destabilized these metal-transporting complexes. Collectively, these results highlight the potential of biotite geochemical signatures as a robust proxy for tracing the full magmatic evolution of highly fractionated porphyry systems.

item.title: Geochemical and petrogenetic characteristics of Miocene adakite-like rocks in Kashan, Central Iran: Implications for post-collisional crustal evolution
item.dc:creator: Tayebeh Khaksar
Ali Reza Davoudian
Shu-Guang Song
Nahid Shabanian
item.description: Early to Middle Miocene volcanic rocks in southern Kashan, part of the central Urumieh–Dokhtar Magmatic Arc (UDMA), offer insights into adakitic-like magmatism and post-collisional crustal evolution in Iran. The suite mainly consists of andesite and rhyodacite with medium- to high-K calc-alkaline affinities, forming lava flows and domes. Zircon U–Pb dating gave ages of 18.2 ± 0.5 Ma for andesite and 14.9 ± 0.3 Ma for rhyodacite, indicating ongoing magmatic activity. These rocks exhibit characteristic adakitic geochemistry, including high Sr/Y (56–99) and La/Yb (20–72), low Y (5–13 ppm) and Yb (0.31–1.52 ppm), and no significant negative Eu anomalies. Sr–Nd isotopes ( 87 Sr/ 86 Sr = 0.7051–0.7062; εNd = −0.27 to −2.15) suggest a source close to bulk Earth composition. Enrichment in LILE and LREE, depletion in HREE, and trace element ratios (high Rb/Sr, high La/Sm, low Nb/U) indicate derivation from partial melting of a thickened mafic lower crust rather than a subducted slab. Geochemical and isotopic data imply progressive crustal thickening to approximately 40–50 km during the Early Cenozoic, linked to the Arabia–Eurasia collision. Slab break-off likely triggered asthenospheric upwelling, providing the heat necessary for lower crustal melting. The resulting magmas underwent minor fractional crystallization and limited crustal assimilation. These findings underscore the role of post-collisional geodynamics in producing adakitic-like magmas and improve understanding of crustal thickening and lithospheric evolution in continental collision zones. Published: 2026-05-27T07:00:44+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.568 (consider)

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

Early to Middle Miocene volcanic rocks in southern Kashan, part of the central Urumieh–Dokhtar Magmatic Arc (UDMA), offer insights into adakitic-like magmatism and post-collisional crustal evolution in Iran. The suite mainly consists of andesite and rhyodacite with medium- to high-K calc-alkaline affinities, forming lava flows and domes. Zircon U–Pb dating gave ages of 18.2 ± 0.5 Ma for andesite and 14.9 ± 0.3 Ma for rhyodacite, indicating ongoing magmatic activity. These rocks exhibit characteristic adakitic geochemistry, including high Sr/Y (56–99) and La/Yb (20–72), low Y (5–13 ppm) and Yb (0.31–1.52 ppm), and no significant negative Eu anomalies. Sr–Nd isotopes ( 87 Sr/ 86 Sr = 0.7051–0.7062; εNd = −0.27 to −2.15) suggest a source close to bulk Earth composition. Enrichment in LILE and LREE, depletion in HREE, and trace element ratios (high Rb/Sr, high La/Sm, low Nb/U) indicate derivation from partial melting of a thickened mafic lower crust rather than a subducted slab. Geochemical and isotopic data imply progressive crustal thickening to approximately 40–50 km during the Early Cenozoic, linked to the Arabia–Eurasia collision. Slab break-off likely triggered asthenospheric upwelling, providing the heat necessary for lower crustal melting. The resulting magmas underwent minor fractional crystallization and limited crustal assimilation. These findings underscore the role of post-collisional geodynamics in producing adakitic-like magmas and improve understanding of crustal thickening and lithospheric evolution in continental collision zones.

item.title: Nature and timing of W-Sn mineralization in the Xin’anzi Deposit, Nanling Region: constraints from LA-ICP-MS cassiterite U-Pb geochronology and trace element composition
item.dc:creator: Gen-Wen He
Pei Ni
Wen-Sheng Li
Lin-Lin Peng
Gua-Hua Wang
Xin-Yue Liang
Jia-Hui Yan
Kai-Han Zhang
Li-Na Zhao
item.description: The Nanling region is globally renowned as a key district for vein-type W-Sn mineralization. The recently discovered Xin’anzi W-Sn deposit is located within this region, yet its metallogenic timing, genetic relationship with concealed granites, and ore-forming fluid evolution remain poorly constrained. These uncertainties represent major obstacles to regional exploration and metallogenic modeling. To resolve these issues, this study presents integrated data from LA-ICP-MS zircon U-Pb dating, cassiterite U-Pb geochronology, in situ trace element analysis of cassiterite, and zircon Hf isotope geochemistry. Zircon U-Pb dating yields a weighted mean age of 163.0 ± 0.82 Ma for the concealed granite, constraining its emplacement to the Early Yanshanian. Cassiterite U-Pb dating, applied for the first time in this deposit, yields a precise metallogenic age of 160.3 ± 0.92 Ma. This age is consistent with the granite emplacement age within analytical uncertainty, confirming a genetic link between magmatism and mineralization. Zircon Hf isotopic compositions (εHf(t) = -13.8 to −10.4; TDM 2 = 1862–2075 Ma) indicate that the granite was generated by partial melting of Paleoproterozoic continental crust, and is classified as a highly fractionated peraluminous S-type granite belonging to the regional metallogenic granite association. In situ trace element analysis of dark brown and light brown zones in cassiterite reveals a three-stage ore-forming fluid evolution: (1) an initial high-temperature, low-oxygen-fugacity fluid enriched in Nb-Ta-W; (2) episodic fluctuations in temperature and fO 2 , leading to alternating deposition of Fe-V-rich and Nb-Ta-rich domains;(3) late-stage low-temperature cooling. Variations in Zr/Hf ratios (25–77) and Fe-W contents of cassiterite further support a magmatic-hydrothermal origin genetically related to granitic magmatism. This study precisely constrains the metallogenic chronology of the Xin’anzi deposit, confirming that it formed during the Middle Jurassic (160–150 Ma) regional W-Sn mineralization event associated with Yanshanian lithospheric extension. These results refine the spatiotemporal framework of Nanling W-Sn metallogeny and indicate significant deep prospectivity (>300 m) along the granite–country rock contact zone for disseminated or greisen-type mineralization. This work provides robust geological constraints for targeted deep exploration and improves the understanding of magmatic-hydrothermal W-Sn systems in intracontinental orogenic belts. Published: 2026-05-28T16:12:15+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.568 (consider)

Relevance: 0.568 (consider)

The Nanling region is globally renowned as a key district for vein-type W-Sn mineralization. The recently discovered Xin’anzi W-Sn deposit is located within this region, yet its metallogenic timing, genetic relationship with concealed granites, and ore-forming fluid evolution remain poorly constrained. To resolve these issues, this study presents integrated data from LA-ICP-MS zircon U-Pb dating, cassiterite U-Pb geochronology, in situ trace element analysis of cassiterite, and zircon Hf isotope geochemistry. Zircon U-Pb dating yields a weighted mean age of 163.0 ± 0.82 Ma for the concealed granite, constraining its emplacement to the Early Yanshanian. Cassiterite U-Pb dating, applied for the first time in this deposit, yields a precise metallogenic age of 160.3 ± 0.92 Ma. This age is consistent with the granite emplacement age within analytical uncertainty, confirming a genetic link between magmatism and mineralization. Zircon Hf isotopic compositions (εHf(t) = -13.8 to −10.4; TDM 2 = 1862–2075 Ma) indicate that the granite was generated by partial melting of Paleoproterozoic continental crust, and is classified as a highly fractionated peraluminous S-type granite belonging to the regional metallogenic granite association. In situ trace element analysis of dark brown and light brown zones in cassiterite reveals a three-stage ore-forming fluid evolution: (1) an initial high-temperature, low-oxygen-fugacity fluid enriched in Nb-Ta-W; (2) episodic fluctuations in temperature and fO 2 , leading to alternating deposition of Fe-V-rich and Nb-Ta-rich domains;(3) late-stage low-temperature cooling. Variations in Zr/Hf ratios (25–77) and Fe-W contents of cassiterite further support a magmatic-hydrothermal origin genetically related to granitic magmatism. This work provides robust geological constraints for targeted deep exploration and improves the understanding of magmatic-hydrothermal W-Sn systems in intracontinental orogenic belts.

item.title: Discrimination of gold deposit types based on big data analysis and machine-learning techniques
item.dc:creator: Lihao Feng
Qingfei Wang
Daniel D. Gregory
Lin Yang
Yunyun Niu
Guodong Zhu
Huajian Li
Hesen Zhao
Weijun Weng
item.description: Classifying the genetic types of gold deposits is essential for effective exploration. This study focuses on developing a classifier that can categorize the diverse types of gold deposits using the trace element contents of pyrite, the most common sulfide mineral in most of these deposits. Based on 13,624 pyrite trace element records from 9 types of gold deposits worldwide, random forest (RF), support vector machine (SVM) and k-nearest neighbor (KNN) classifiers were constructed and evaluated. Comparative analysis identified the RF classifier as the best performing model, exhibiting superior predictive accuracy and robustness. Model interpretation and geochemical validation employed SHAP (feature importance analysis), Spearman correlation (element associations), and UMAP (dimensionality reduction and visualization). Results show distinct classification of deposit types, confirming pyrite trace elements as reliable genetic indicators. Feature importance analysis highlighted As, Te, and Ag as key discriminators. Pyrite from Carlin and orogenic deposits is rich in As and Au, whereas epithermal and VMS types have moderate-to-low Te, As, and Se. Porphyry-related pyrite is low in Sb, As, and Au. In contrast, SEDEX pyrite is enriched in Te, Se, and Bi, and skarn pyrite shows high Zn but low As and Se. These systematic variations suggest that the trace elements signature of pyrite is primarily controlled by fluid-rock interaction processes during mineralization, which are strongly influenced by the composition of the host rocks. Published: 2026-05-29T00:07:34+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.567 (consider)

item.title: Rare earth element partitioning in Hadean zircons establishes granitic magma source
item.dc:creator: Xinyu Zou
Yong Zhao
Xiaocai Shan
Jilian Jiang
Ross N. Mitchell
Yi Zou
Hao Wang
Wei Yang
Zhuosen Yao
Kezhang Qin
Xian-Hua Li
item.description: Deciphering the composition and evolution of Earth’s earliest continental crust commonly relies on reconstructing parental-melt rare earth element (REE) signatures from Hadean zircons. This approach is highly sensitive to zircon–melt REE partition coefficients ( D REE ). However, published zircon D REE values span orders of magnitude, leading to divergent and sometimes contradictory inferences about early crustal compositions. A major cause of this inconsistency is the lack of rigorous criteria for assessing the reliability of natural zircon D REE . Here, we compile a global dataset of natural zircon/bulk-rock pairs and adopt the lattice-strain-based δ K " role="presentation"> δ K δ K deviation index of Zou et al. (2019) to screen for robust D REE . Using the screened dataset, we establish recommended D REE envelopes for high-SiO 2 and low-SiO 2 natural systems. We evaluate recommended high-SiO 2 envelope with D REE estimated from different zircon generations within the Acasta Gneiss Complex. The earliest igneous zircon population yields D REE values that fall largely within this envelope, supporting its applicability to ancient felsic zircon-forming systems. Finally, we apply the recommended high-SiO 2 D REE envelope to reconstruct parental-melt REE patterns for detrital zircons from Jack Hills, Green Sandstone Bed, and Singhbhum Craton. The resulting REE signatures are most consistent with incompatible-element-enriched granitoid compositions than with TTG, Acasta-like, or Icelandic analogues. This integrated mineral-physics and big-data workflow reduces longstanding uncertainty in zircon D REE selection and provides a broadly applicable strategy for constraining plausible partition coefficients across mineral–magma systems. Published: 2026-05-26T10:13:07+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.566 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xinyu Zou; Yong Zhao; Xiaocai Shan; Jilian Jiang; Ross N. Mitchell; Yi Zou; Hao Wang; Wei Yang; Zhuosen Yao; Kezhang Qin; Xian-Hua Li

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120136

Relevance: 0.566 (consider)

Deciphering the composition and evolution of Earth’s earliest continental crust commonly relies on reconstructing parental-melt rare earth element (REE) signatures from Hadean zircons. This approach is highly sensitive to zircon–melt REE partition coefficients ( D REE ). However, published zircon D REE values span orders of magnitude, leading to divergent and sometimes contradictory inferences about early crustal compositions. A major cause of this inconsistency is the lack of rigorous criteria for assessing the reliability of natural zircon D REE . Here, we compile a global dataset of natural zircon/bulk-rock pairs and adopt the lattice-strain-based δ K " role="presentation"> δ K δ K deviation index of Zou et al. (2019) to screen for robust D REE . Using the screened dataset, we establish recommended D REE envelopes for high-SiO 2 and low-SiO 2 natural systems. We evaluate recommended high-SiO 2 envelope with D REE estimated from different zircon generations within the Acasta Gneiss Complex. The earliest igneous zircon population yields D REE values that fall largely within this envelope, supporting its applicability to ancient felsic zircon-forming systems. Finally, we apply the recommended high-SiO 2 D REE envelope to reconstruct parental-melt REE patterns for detrital zircons from Jack Hills, Green Sandstone Bed, and Singhbhum Craton. The resulting REE signatures are most consistent with incompatible-element-enriched granitoid compositions than with TTG, Acasta-like, or Icelandic analogues. This integrated mineral-physics and big-data workflow reduces longstanding uncertainty in zircon D REE selection and provides a broadly applicable strategy for constraining plausible partition coefficients across mineral–magma systems.

item.title: Anomalously Low <sup>3</sup> He/ <sup>4</sup> He and Elevated Heat Flow in the Northern Apennines Explained by Lithospheric Delamination: A Modeling Approach
item.dc:creator: Jean de la Paix Izerumugaba
Anne Battani
Frédéric Mouthereau
Anthony Ranchou‐Peyruse
item.description: Abstract The 3 He/ 4 He ratios measured in 12 CO 2 ‐dominated gas seeps from the Tuscany‐Roman Volcanic Provinces in Italy range between 0.07 and ∼1.6 R a . Helium and δ 13 CO 2 isotope systematics indicate a dominant crustal source with a variable but significantly lower contribution from the mantle. Low He ( 3 He/ 4 He = 0.6–1.6 R a ) and high Sr ( 87 Sr/ 86 Sr = 0.709514–0.710595) isotope systematics of olivine and pyroxene mantle phenocrysts have been previously interpreted as related to the addition of a crustal signature into a HIMU‐type magma (6.7 ± 0.4 R a ) in the mantle by subduction of continental crust fluids and rocks, thus explaining the much lower 3 He/ 4 He ratio compared to typical oceanic subduction zones. This is consistent with the interpretation that CO 2 is derived from the decarbonation of limestones at mantle depth, as corroborated by the presence of crustal carbonate melts within the low‐velocity mantle wedge. Here, we explore a model in which crustal contamination expresses the delamination of the continental lithosphere of the (previously subducted) Adria microplate, as previously inferred from geophysics and mechanical modeling. We present coupled thermal and helium isotopic modeling that accounts for radiogenic heat and 4 He production in the U, Th, and K‐rich delaminated continental crust and in the variably aged (depending on SCLM age and He residence time) 4 He‐rich subcontinental lithospheric mantle. Our models support a delamination tectonic scenario in which the unusually low 3 He/ 4 He values and high heat flow data are reproduced by introducing a 15–20 km—thick continental crust into the mantle. Published: 2026-05-27T11:09:21+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.561 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jean de la Paix Izerumugaba; Anne Battani; Frédéric Mouthereau; Anthony Ranchou‐Peyruse

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012692

Relevance: 0.561 (consider)

Abstract The 3 He/ 4 He ratios measured in 12 CO 2 ‐dominated gas seeps from the Tuscany‐Roman Volcanic Provinces in Italy range between 0.07 and ∼1.6 R a . Helium and δ 13 CO 2 isotope systematics indicate a dominant crustal source with a variable but significantly lower contribution from the mantle. Low He ( 3 He/ 4 He = 0.6–1.6 R a ) and high Sr ( 87 Sr/ 86 Sr = 0.709514–0.710595) isotope systematics of olivine and pyroxene mantle phenocrysts have been previously interpreted as related to the addition of a crustal signature into a HIMU‐type magma (6.7 ± 0.4 R a ) in the mantle by subduction of continental crust fluids and rocks, thus explaining the much lower 3 He/ 4 He ratio compared to typical oceanic subduction zones. This is consistent with the interpretation that CO 2 is derived from the decarbonation of limestones at mantle depth, as corroborated by the presence of crustal carbonate melts within the low‐velocity mantle wedge. Here, we explore a model in which crustal contamination expresses the delamination of the continental lithosphere of the (previously subducted) Adria microplate, as previously inferred from geophysics and mechanical modeling. We present coupled thermal and helium isotopic modeling that accounts for radiogenic heat and 4 He production in the U, Th, and K‐rich delaminated continental crust and in the variably aged (depending on SCLM age and He residence time) 4 He‐rich subcontinental lithospheric mantle. Our models support a delamination tectonic scenario in which the unusually low 3 He/ 4 He values and high heat flow data are reproduced by introducing a 15–20 km—thick continental crust into the mantle.
